es.meaning: To be unable to wait; to be dying for something.
es.example: No veo la hora de que lleguen las vacaciones.
es.exampleTranslation: I can't wait for the holidays to arrive.
es.regionNote: Common in LatAm (MX/AR); in Spain 'estar deseando' is more natural. A rare pair where the LATAM side matches Italian and the Peninsular side doesn't — reviewer to confirm distribution.

it.meaning: To be unable to wait; to look forward eagerly.
it.exampleTranslation: I can't wait to see you.

bridge.note: A twin with a twist in the joinery: both can't see the hour, but Italian follows with di + infinitive (non vedo l'ora DI vederti) while Spanish takes de que + subjunctive when subjects differ. Same idiom, different plumbing underneath — and the Italian is among the most-used phrases in the language.

interference.es_to_it: The connector: 'non vedo l'ora che vieni' fails twice — it wants di + infinitive (same subject) or che + SUBJUNCTIVE (different subject): non vedo l'ora che tu venga.
interference.it_to_es: Italians say 'no veo la hora de verte' — perfect in LatAm; in Spain it may read as an italianism.

review.notes: Discovered via FluentU list (Joe, 2026-07-16); all content authored fresh, ES pairing by Claude — verify.
